# Lab 4: Control Structures in Java

## Mastering Conditional Statements and Loops

### Objective
In this lab, you will learn and practice using control structures in Java, including if-else statements, switch statements, for loops, while loops, and do-while loops. By the end of this lab, you will be able to write efficient and readable Java code that incorporates these control structures.

### Prerequisites
- Basic understanding of Java syntax and data types
- Familiarity with Java Development Kit (JDK) and a Java IDE

---

### Step 1: If-Else Statements

Create a new Java project in your preferred IDE and write a program that uses if-else statements to determine whether a given number is positive, negative, or zero.
```java
// Import necessary packages
import java.util.Scanner;

public class IfElseExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Declare and initialize variables
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a number: ");
        int number = scanner.nextInt();
        scanner.close();

        // Use if-else statements to determine the sign of the number
        if (number > 0) {
            System.out.println("The number is positive.");
        } else if (number < 0) {
            System.out.println("The number is negative.");
        } else {
            System.out.println("The number is zero.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Result:**
The program will prompt the user to enter a number and then print whether the number is positive, negative, or zero.

---

### Step 2: Switch Statements

Modify the previous program to use a switch statement to determine the day of the week based on a given day number (1-7).
```java
// Import necessary packages
import java.util.Scanner;

public class SwitchExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Declare and initialize variables
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a day number (1-7): ");
        int dayNumber = scanner.nextInt();
        scanner.close();

        // Use a switch statement to determine the day of the week
        switch (dayNumber) {
            case 1:
                System.out.println("Monday");
                break;
            case 2:
                System.out.println("Tuesday");
                break;
            case 3:
                System.out.println("Wednesday");
                break;
            case 4:
                System.out.println("Thursday");
                break;
            case 5:
                System.out.println("Friday");
                break;
            case 6:
                System.out.println("Saturday");
                break;
            case 7:
                System.out.println("Sunday");
                break;
            default:
                System.out.println("Invalid day number");
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Result:**
The program will prompt the user to enter a day number and then print the corresponding day of the week.

---

### Step 3: For Loops

Write a program that uses a for loop to print the numbers from 1 to 10.
```java
// Import necessary packages

public class ForLoopExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Use a for loop to print the numbers from 1 to 10
        for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
            System.out.println(i);
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Result:**
The program will print the numbers from 1 to 10.

---

### Step 4: While Loops

Modify the previous program to use a while loop to print the numbers from 1 to 10.
```java
// Import necessary packages

public class WhileLoopExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Declare and initialize variables
        int i = 1;

        // Use a while loop to print the numbers from 1 to 10
        while (i <= 10) {
            System.out.println(i);
            i++;
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Result:**
The program will print the numbers from 1 to 10.

---

### Step 5: Do-While Loops

Write a program that uses a do-while loop to print the numbers from 1 to 10.
```java
// Import necessary packages

public class DoWhileLoopExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Declare and initialize variables
        int i = 1;

        // Use a do-while loop to print the numbers from 1 to 10
        do {
            System.out.println(i);
            i++;
        } while (i <= 10);
    }
}
```

**Expected Result:**
The program will print the numbers from 1 to 10.

---

### Summary
In this lab, you learned and practiced using control structures in Java, including if-else statements, switch statements, for loops, while loops, and do-while loops. You wrote programs that demonstrated the use of these control structures to perform various tasks.

### Optional Challenge
Write a program that uses control structures to simulate a simple game, such as a guessing game or a rock-paper-scissors game. Experiment with different control structures and logic to create a more complex and engaging game.
